We usually enjoy heist films, but this one was disappointing for us. It was unnecessarily complicated, and the movie took itself so seriously that it lacked any sense of humor. That said, the cast was excellent and the production value was extraordinarily high. In 4K on our home theater system, the sound and images were impressive.

Inception is a masterpiece of a blockbuster and firmly in the upper echelon of Christopher Nolan’s exceptional body of work. Technically, the film is a marvel, boasting stunning CGI and some truly incredible cinematography that elevates every set piece. It also features one of Hans Zimmer’s greatest film scores, bookended by the all-time classic Time, which leaves a lasting emotional imprint. The concept is wildly creative, and you genuinely need to think while watching to fully appreciate the layers and ambition of the story. I do have some issues with the amount of shaky cam in certain action sequences, but I’ll happily let that slide because everything else is so good—definitely watch it, and if possible, see it in IMAX.
This is such a cool film that studies the nature of psychology and dreams. I had a hard time following it in the beginning, but as the movie progressed th story started to fall into place.
This movie is trippy. But never the less, it is amazing!

Inception creates a genre of its own blending suspense, familiarity, and an uncanny ability to place the viewer directly in the protagonist’s shoes. This mix hits the mark with every rewatch, and it’s one of those films that truly reveals itself only after being watched multiple times. It delivers a level of awareness few films can match, provocative, immersive, and quietly intimidating, making each return feel less like watching a movie and more like stepping back into a state of mind from the feeling of a free fall to the threat of despair, leaving one to question: what is my limbo? And once that question is planted, it doesn’t let go.
I envy every single member of the new generation who will experience the thrills of watching this masterpiece for the very first time.

Greetings from Lithuania. Now that was something... I simply run out off words after watching this one for the first time in 2010. This is going to sound very familiar, but this film was insanely brilliant. This was probably the best experience i had in a movies - ever, at the time. As much as "Avatar" was breathtaking experience in visuals, "Inception" is a breathtaking experience in storytelling. Everything in this movie works on the level of masterpiece - and i mean - everything! I didn't completely understood all aspects of the film in the first viewing (and i don't thing anyone did) - but this film blew my away. As much as i love "The Dark Knight", I think "Inception" is better film. Watch this film, way you still can, because after 20 years, this is going to the be classic like "2001" and "Blade Runner" - it's THAT good. By the way, the zero-gravity scene in the hotel so reminded my of "2001" - the music, cinematography, the feeling... the masterpiece.

Inception is a thrilling ride that mixes action, emotion, and mind-bending ideas about dreams and reality. Leonardo DiCaprio leads a talented cast as a skilled thief who enters people’s dreams to steal secrets. The story is complex but rewarding, and the visuals are stunning, from city streets folding over themselves to zero-gravity fight scenes. The film keeps you thinking long after it ends, questioning what is real and what is imagined. Hans Zimmer’s score adds intensity and emotion, making every scene feel epic. Inception is both an exciting action movie and a thought-provoking puzzle. If you love movies that challenge your mind while keeping you on the edge of your seat, Inception is a must-watch.

"Inception" Where do I start. It is typical Cristopher Nolan movie with his storytelling, foreshadowing what the twist will be in the middle of the movie, excellent cinematography, beautiful visual effects and the theme song from Hans Zimmer is just WOW. Christopher Nolan always relies on the naturalism and that is why he tries to not use CGI, like in the hallway scene or the explosions in Paris's Café scene, the explosion of the winyer fortress, the Hotel's bar rotation and the fact that he made the actors actually stay in the van while being underwater. The concept about this movie is brilliant. The fact that you can live in a dream and control it, that you can make everything you can't in the real world. The movie had action, feeling, it was tought-provoking, thrilling and had perfect cast with great performances put on. I can't ignore the fact that it has problems like poor character development (mostly for the female characters (Ariadne especially)), the fact that he uses a dead wife story (again) and the fact that the movie brings confusion in the viewers that probably stops them from feeling it. I love the hidden details in it because you can watch the movie 5 times and always find something new and something you don't fully understand. Cristopher Nolan always says that his movies are not to be understood but to feel them and I felt this movie especially with this iconic ending. MY FAVORITE MOVIE EVER. Sorry if I had mistakes in the grammar or the writing. English isn't my native language.

Visuals: 10/10 – Mind-bending set pieces, spinning hallways, folding cities… iconic cinematography from start to finish. Soundtrack: 10/10 – Hans Zimmer’s score is legendary. "Time" alone carries half the movie’s emotional weight. Concept/Worldbuilding: 10/10 – Dream layers, shared dreaming, time dilation—one of the smartest and most original ideas in modern cinema. Story: 9/10 – Complex but rewarding. A heist movie wrapped inside a psychological drama about grief and guilt. Performances: 9/10 – DiCaprio leads a strong cast. Hardy, Murphy, and Page all bring depth and personality. Overall, 9.4/10
